finding: Processing information for survival relevance produced superior retention compared to other deep-processing conditions including imagery, pleasantness, self-reference, intentional learning, and contextually rich scenarios.
Both wanting what one has and having what one wants independently contributed to overall satisfaction; each partially mediated effects of different personal dispositions on satisfaction.
Exposure to deterministic framing increased unethical behavior, mediated by reduced belief in personal agency; exposure to free-will framing prevented this unethical response.

Change detection performance declined with stimulus complexity in both short and long interstimulus intervals, suggesting auditory change detection relies on different mechanisms than visual change detection.

Spatial distance priming decreased emotional engagement with media, lowered estimates of stimulus magnitude, and weakened attachment judgments compared to spatial closeness priming, independent of self-relevance.
Individuals preferred to increase an unpleasant emotional state when anticipating situations where that state would improve performance, demonstrating instrumental goal overrides hedonic preference.

Induced social disconnection increased anthropomorphization of nonhuman agents and belief in anthropomorphized religious agents; this effect was specific to social disconnection rather than general negative affect.

In intergroup competition scenarios, contributions were directed almost exclusively toward within-group cooperation rather than intergroup competition, with communication increasing intragroup but not intergroup cooperation.
Word-order constraints in a language modulated phonological activation during speech production, with effects appearing in the output position required by the language's word order rules.
Partnered women preferred attached men at low fertility but shifted to preferring single high-quality men at peak fertility, consistent with a strategic mate-choice adjustment.
Prototypical object views enhanced target detection in the lower visual field via dorsal stream processing; action-relevant targets showed reduced fixation durations in the lower field.
Activating the concept of cleanliness or physical cleansing after negative emotion reduced severity of moral judgments, reversing the typical effect of emotion-driven moral judgment.
Extremal edges (self-occlusion projections) functioned as powerful depth and figure-ground cues, outperforming classical cues such as size and surroundedness in determining perceived proximity.

Cross-national personality self-reports and peer reports failed to correlate with behavioral predictors, whereas perceptions of national character did, indicating measurement invalidity in comparative personality research.
Individuals high in dominance exhibited faster spatial attention to vertical space dimensions compared to those low in dominance, supporting metaphor-representation of conceptual hierarchies.
Response conflicts increased goal shielding on the conflict trial itself, resulting in greater neglect of task-irrelevant prospective cues rather than solely producing post-conflict adaptation.
Evolutionarily relevant threat stimuli were detected more rapidly than nonthreatening stimuli in visual search tasks across both preschool children and adults.
Object words activated perceptual simulations that oriented attention toward the spatial location where the denoted objects typically occur, interfering with target detection in those regions.

Causal attribution strength depended on whether the counterfactual alternative (default mutation) would have prevented the outcome; causality was judged greater when the alternative would have undone the result.
Observers learned to compress working memory representations by exploiting statistical regularities in input, improving memory capacity within the limits of an optimal encoding scheme.
